planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown stock in spring in moist to well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican elderberry establishes rapidly and may produce its first berry clusters within one to two years of planting. Hardwood cuttings taken in winter root reliably in moist potting mix or directly in the ground. For heaviest fruit set, plant at least two individuals of different seed origins to encourage cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained to moderately moist soil; Mexican elderberry is more drought-tolerant than eastern common elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) once established and adapts well to the neutral to alkaline soils of the Southwest, California foothills, and the intermountain West. Sambucus mexicana (syn. Sambucus nigra subsp. cerulea) is the native elderberry of California, the Southwest, and the Pacific Northwest mountains, forming a large, fast-growing, multi-stemmed shrub or small tree with massive, flat-topped flower clusters (corymbs) of creamy-white blossoms in late spring that are irresistible to native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. The flowers are followed by heavy drooping clusters of small, powdery-blue to blue-black berries in late summer — excellent for elderberry syrup, jelly, wine, and traditional medicine. CAUTION: cook berries before eating; raw berries, bark, and leaves contain cyanogenic glycosides. An outstanding wildlife plant — the berries are consumed by dozens of bird species including waxwings, robins, and thrushes.
sources: USDA Forest Service Fire Effects Information System

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; American beautyberry establishes readily and grows quickly, typically reaching 4–6 feet in its first few seasons. Softwood cuttings taken in summer root easily under intermittent mist.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil; American beautyberry tolerates a wide range of soil types and performs well in woodland edges, shade gardens, and naturalistic borders. Callicarpa americana is a deciduous native shrub of the southeastern United States and one of the most visually arresting plants in the native landscape — in late summer and fall it is covered in tight, globe-shaped clusters of vivid magenta to violet-purple berries arranged in rings directly along the arching stems, creating a spectacle unlike any other shrub. The berries persist well after leaf drop and provide outstanding late-season food for mockingbirds, robins, catbirds, and other wildlife. Prune hard in late winter (to within 6–12 inches of the ground) to encourage vigorous new growth and the heaviest berry production, as berries form on new wood; without pruning the shrub can become leggy over time.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown stock in spring or fall in virtually any soil type in full sun to full shade; yaupon holly is one of the most adaptable native shrubs in North America, tolerating drought, flooding, poor soils, salt spray, and heavy shade. It is dioecious — plant at least one male for every 3–6 females to ensure berry production on female plants.

directions: Plant in full sun to full shade in virtually any well-drained to periodically wet soil — yaupon holly is among the most stress-tolerant of all native North American shrubs, thriving in sand, clay, limestone caliche, brackish coastal soils, and dry urban sites once established. Ilex vomitoria is the only native North American plant known to contain significant quantities of caffeine, and its dried leaves were used by Indigenous peoples of the Southeast to brew a stimulating ceremonial tea called 'Black Drink' — today it is being explored commercially as a sustainable source of caffeine for teas and beverages. Female plants produce masses of small, translucent-red (rarely yellow) berries that persist through winter and provide critical food for cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, bluebirds, and many other bird species. Outstanding cultivars range from large screening shrubs to weeping forms, dwarf groundcovers, and topiary specimens, giving landscape designers exceptional versatility with a genuinely native, wildlife-supporting plant.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; possumhaw is dioecious — you must plant at least one male within 50 feet of female plants to ensure the heavy berry display. The cultivar 'Red Cascade' and 'Council Fire' are outstanding female selections with brilliant berry color.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically wet soil in full sun to partial shade; possumhaw adapts naturally to floodplains and stream banks and tolerates periodic inundation better than most landscape shrubs. Ilex decidua is a native deciduous holly of the southeastern and south-central US that delivers one of the most spectacular winter berry displays of any North American shrub — after the leaves drop in fall, the bare stems are completely covered in dense clusters of brilliant red to orange berries that persist through February, providing vivid color and abundant wildlife food throughout the dormant season. The berries are heavily utilized by cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, and bluebirds during winter irruptions. A male pollinator plant (such as Ilex decidua 'Red Escort' or a male I. opaca) within pollinating distance is essential for berry set on female plants. Outstanding for naturalistic borders, rain gardens, and stream-bank plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring at the margins of a pond, stream, or water garden with the crown at or just below the waterline; pickerelweed grows best in 2–6 inches of standing water but will establish in saturated mud at water's edge. Space divisions 18–24 inches apart in containers of heavy aquatic soil submerged just below the water surface, or plant directly into the soft sediment of a natural pond margin.

directions: Plant at the shallow margins of a pond, rain garden, or water feature in 2–6 inches of standing water or saturated soil in full sun to partial shade; pickerelweed is a native emergent aquatic perennial found naturally in marshes, sluggish streams, lake margins, and ditches across eastern North America and is one of the most ornamental and wildlife-valuable native aquatic plants available. Pontederia cordata produces bold, heart-shaped, glossy dark-green leaves and upright spikes of violet-blue, two-lipped flowers from June through October — one of the longest-blooming of any native aquatic — that are excellent nectar sources for bumble bees, honey bees, skippers, and swallowtails. The small, hard fruits that follow are eaten by ducks and other waterfowl; the plant also provides critical cover and nesting substrate for frogs, fish, and aquatic insects. Spreads by rhizomes to form attractive colonies at pond edges and can be easily divided every few years to share or control spread.

planting.note: Plant corms or rhizome divisions in spring into saturated mud or shallow water up to 12 inches deep; push corms just below the surface of heavy aquatic soil and keep continuously submerged or wet throughout the growing season. Broadleaf arrowhead spreads by stolons to produce new corms at their tips — harvest corms in fall after the foliage dies back.

directions: Plant corms in heavy aquatic soil in saturated mud or standing water 2–12 inches deep in full sun; broadleaf arrowhead thrives in freshwater marshes, pond edges, slow streams, and ditches across most of North America and is both a highly ornamental aquatic perennial and a traditional Indigenous food plant. Sagittaria latifolia produces bold, arrowhead-shaped leaves on long petioles rising 1–4 feet above the water surface, and attractive whorled racemes of bright-white, three-petaled flowers with yellow stamens from midsummer through early fall — providing valuable nectar for native bees. The starchy, potato-like corms produced at the tips of underground stolons were a critical food source for many Native American and First Nations peoples and were gathered in enormous quantities, especially by Plains tribes — Meriwether Lewis and William Clark documented their use extensively; the corms can be baked, boiled, roasted, or dried and ground into flour. Outstanding for naturalizing pond margins and rain gardens while providing important food and cover for ducks (hence 'duck potato'), muskrats, and other wetland wildlife.
